claim: SPS learns forward and auxiliary backward stochastic paths by minimizing a path-space variational free energy that is also interpreted as an entropy-production upper bound.
allowed_short_sentence: SPS learns a low-irreversibility path between prior and target.
boundary: This is the formulation in SPS v1; do not replace it by terminal-density KL training.

claim: SPS uses an extended-space independence Metropolis-Hastings step to correct learned independent path proposals.
allowed_short_sentence: The learned proposal is followed by an exactness gate.
boundary: Do not call the uncorrected SPS output exact; the paper itself reports tail and susceptibility deviations before IMH.

claim: The closest methodological ancestors are path-space stochastic control/diffusion samplers and nonequilibrium transport methods, not only ordinary normalizing flows.
allowed_short_sentence: SPS sits at the intersection of path-space control and nonequilibrium transport.
boundary: This is a synthesis from direct SPS references and shared path-measure objectives, not a claim that the algorithms are identical.

claim: A parallel lattice lineage combines normalizing-flow maps with Metropolis, reweighting or Jarzynski corrections, then extends to gauge, fermion and stochastic-flow settings.
allowed_short_sentence: The lattice lineage repeatedly pairs learned proposals with a correction mechanism.
boundary: The methods differ in proposal construction, target system and scaling; shared correction logic does not imply equal performance.

claim: Diffusion models do not automatically remove critical slowing down: in a controlled Gaussian O(n) analysis, one-layer training and generation inherit it, while depth plus physical locality changes the reported scaling.
allowed_short_sentence: Diffusion helps only when architecture and physics match the slow modes.
boundary: The quadratic-to-logarithmic result is established in the Gaussian O(n to infinity) setting, not universally for interacting lattice theories.

claim: Promising scale-transfer mechanisms include cross-volume convolutional scores, coarse-to-fine factorization and localized boundary-defect transformations.
allowed_short_sentence: Transfer, multiscale structure and locality are three distinct routes to larger lattices.
boundary: Evidence is method- and benchmark-specific; realistic full-QCD scaling is not yet established.

claim: A fair efficiency claim must include training, proposal generation, correction/reweighting and observable-level uncertainty, not ESS alone.
allowed_short_sentence: Efficiency means physical error per total compute, not a single ESS number.
boundary: The literature supplies components of this standard but not one uniform cross-paper benchmark.

claim: Mode, tail and operator-sector diagnostics are needed because aggregate losses or ESS can miss physically important errors.
allowed_short_sentence: A sampler can look good globally and still miss the modes that matter.
boundary: Diagnostics identify risk; they do not by themselves correct a sampler.

claim: Most strong demonstrations still use 2D scalar or Abelian gauge systems; a small set of 4D SU(3) studies is emerging, while dynamical-fermion, continuum and full-cost evidence remains limited.
allowed_short_sentence: The field is active, but realistic lattice-QCD evidence is still sparse.
boundary: Do not describe the approach as mature or proven at production QCD scale.

claim: The most defensible SPS follow-up directions are stronger support/infrared diagnostics, architecture-locality studies, exact-correction efficiency, cross-volume or multiscale transfer, and gauge/fermion extensions.
allowed_short_sentence: The next step is to close correctness, scaling and physics-cost loops together.
boundary: These are literature-grounded directions, not results already achieved by SPS.
